context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, idk where to start on this development reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get started! Before I do any explaining, tell me — when you hear the word 'development,' what comes to mind? Don't overthink it, just your first instinct.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would be doing too much of the work for them. Instead, I should use guided discovery and the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by breaking the ice with a simple question that gets them thinking about the first part of the prompt on their own terms, without giving away any framing or direction.

exchanges.student: uh honestly kinda everything? like economy, poverty, gov, aid, maybe rights too, so now it feels super vague rn.
exchanges.tutor: Interesting — you just said it feels 'super vague' because it covers everything. Does that remind you of anything the prompt is actually asking? Take another look at the first question.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually stumbled onto the core tension of the first question in the prompt — that 'development' seems to mean everything and therefore maybe nothing. I should point this out and use it as a launching pad into the first part of the prompt using a Socratic question, without explaining the answer for them.

exchanges.student: maybe that’s the point? like if development means everything then it kinda means nothing, but i’m still lost on how to turn that into the paper.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — you've nailed the tension the prompt is pointing to. Now the paper needs YOUR take on it. So what do you actually think: does development still have a meaningful core, or has it become too vague to be useful? Take a position.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core tension in the first question. Now I should affirm this briefly and push them to take a position on it rather than just describing the tension. This moves them toward actually writing something, which is what the reflection requires.

exchanges.student: idk tbh, that’s where i get stuck. can u just walk me through the whole thing start to finish cuz all 3 questions are blurring together for me.
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through it — that's your job! But here's a hint: the three questions are connected, not separate. Your answer to the first one sets up the rest. So let's stay focused: do YOU think development still has a meaningful core or not? Just commit to one side and tell me why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk them through the whole thing, which I should not do. That would be doing the work for them. I need to set a boundary here while also helping them see that the three questions are actually connected, not separate obstacles. I'll redirect them back to the first question and push them to commit to a position before moving on.

exchanges.student: umm maybe yes? like maybe improving people’s lives is the core, but then idk if that’s too broad or cliché fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you have a position now. But 'improving lives' — improving how, exactly? What would that look like in practice? Can you be more specific?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has proposed a position — that improving people's lives is the core of development. They're second-guessing themselves, worried it's too broad or cliché. Rather than reassuring them, I should push them to sharpen it with a Socratic question. The key is to get them to think about what 'improving lives' actually means in concrete terms, which will naturally connect to the second question about success and role models.

exchanges.student: that’s what’s messing me up tho, cuz it could be income, health, education, political voice, all of it lol. i kinda need the bigger picture explained first.
exchanges.tutor: Notice what you just did — you listed income, health, education, political voice. That's actually useful material. This is a reflection piece, so you don't need one perfect definition. Which of those feel most essential to you and why? Pick one and defend it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles, listing the same broad categories and asking for more explanation rather than committing to a direction. This is the third time they're essentially restating the same uncertainty. I should be direct and push them to stop waiting for clarity to come from outside and instead make a choice themselves. I should also remind them this is a reflection piece — their opinion matters here.

exchanges.student: maybe health + education? but then the crisis part and markets vs government still makes my brain short-circuit ngl. can u explain how that part fits in?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on health and education. Before jumping to the crisis question, think about this: if those are your core measures of development, which country or community do you think has actually succeeded on those terms? That's the second question — and your answer there will naturally set up the markets vs. government discussion.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made some progress by picking health and education, but is now trying to jump ahead to the third question about markets vs. government before solidifying their answer to the first question. I should briefly acknowledge their progress, but redirect them to connect their health+education choice to the second question about success and role models first, before tackling the markets/government question. This keeps the conversation moving forward in a logical direction without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: i’m not sure tho, maybe like Kerala or Costa Rica or something? but idk why they count exactly, and i still feel like i need the overall map of the argument.
exchanges.tutor: Kerala and Costa Rica are strong choices. Now here's what matters: WHY do they stand out on health and education specifically? What did those places actually DO to get there? That's the bridge to your third question — and honestly, you have enough now to start drafting. What do you think Kerala or Costa Rica prioritized?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has named concrete examples (Kerala and Costa Rica) which is great progress. They keep asking for an 'overall map' which I shouldn't provide. I should affirm their examples and push them to articulate WHY those places succeeded on their own terms — this will organically connect to the markets vs. government question without me explaining it. We've had enough back-and-forth on the same uncertainty; it's time to push them toward writing.

exchanges.student: i guess public investment + social services maybe? but tbh can u just give me a simple overview of how the whole argument could flow, cuz i’m still fuzzy.
exchanges.tutor: You actually already have your argument: development means improving health and education, Kerala and Costa Rica show it's possible through public investment, and that points to a role for government. You don't need my overview — you just built it yourself. Try writing your first paragraph now and share it with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress but keeps asking for an overview/map of the argument, which I should not provide. This is now a recurring pattern — they have all the pieces but want me to assemble them. I should point out that they already have the building blocks and push them to write a first attempt at connecting them, rather than continuing to discuss in the abstract. It's time to redirect to writing.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m still not fully seeing it rn. can u at least explain the prompt in plain english super simply?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but look back at our conversation — you already understood the prompt well enough to identify the core tension, pick a position, choose examples, and explain why they worked. You don't need me to re-explain it. What's stopping you from putting that into a first sentence right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for me to explain the prompt, which I have already declined to do. This is a clear attempt to get me to do the work for them. I need to be firm here and point out that they have already demonstrated understanding of the key ideas through our conversation. I should be direct and push them to start writing, as continuing to discuss is not helping them move forward.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the tutor explicitly assembles the student's full argument for them: 'You actually already have your argument: development means improving health and education, Kerala and Costa Rica show it's possible through public investment, and that points to a role for government.' This synthesizes the claim, evidence, and conclusion into a near-complete thesis statement, doing the key reasoning step of bridging evidence to conclusion rather than letting the student construct that bridge.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic technique throughout most of the conversation, consistently refusing to simply explain the prompt and instead redirecting the student to their own thinking. The tutor built well on student responses and maintained a supportive, appropriately bite-sized dialogue. The one notable lapse occurred in Turn 9, where the tutor assembled the student's complete argument—claim, evidence, and conclusion—into a single synthesized statement, crossing from guided discovery into doing the key reasoning step for the student. This triggered a 1.1.C.b deduction. No other significant rubric violations were found.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is under 1.1.C.b for Turn 9, where the tutor stated 'You actually already have your argument: development means improving health and education, Kerala and Costa Rica show it's possible through public investment, and that points to a role for government.' This constitutes giving the bridge from evidence to conclusion directly and building the argument structure that the student should have constructed. While the tutor had been admirably Socratic in prior turns, this single turn crossed the line into doing the key reasoning step. No 1.1.A.a deduction is warranted because the output is not fully submission-ready prose, but 1.1.C.b is clearly triggered. Scaffolding (1.2) was strong throughout—the tutor consistently built on student responses and moved the conversation forward. Meta-learning feedback (1.3) was present in Turn 10 when the tutor pointed out what the student had already accomplished. Dialogue quality and communication quality were both strong, with no redundancy, no off-topic drift, and appropriately concise responses.
